text: This video is an example of a fully robotic two-stage hepatectomy.
The patient is a seventy-six-year-old female with an obstructive tumor of the right colon with bilobarosynchronous colorectal metastasis.
Between stage one and two, a right purple vein embolization was performed.

The surgeon performs a segment IV and V resection of the liver, along with an en bloc cholecystectomy, using robotic instruments to excise the specified liver segments and gallbladder in a single surgical maneuver.
The surgeon marks the borders of liver segment IVb, preparing for resection.
The surgeon performs a cholecystectomy, removing the gallbladder from its attachments using robotic instruments.
The surgeon applies a Pringle maneuver using the Hohen Loop technique to temporarily occlude hepatic inflow before proceeding with the liver transaction.
The surgeon begins the resection of segment IVb, with visible demarcation of the anatomical borders and initial incision into the liver tissue.
Following the resection, the surgeon removes the Pringle maneuver and achieves hemostasis using bipolar cautery and hemostatic agents, ensuring the surgical field is free of bleeding.
Two months after the first stage and right portal vein embolization, a robotic right hemohepatectomy was performed.
The surgeon begins the Hiller dissection, focusing on the anatomical structures involved in the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon observes the liver hilum, which appears hostile with tense adhesions, likely resulting from previous liver surgery.
The surgeon encircles the right hepatic duct, applies clips to secure it, and then transects the duct.
The surgeon encircles the right hepatic artery, applies clips, and transects the vessel, completing the dissection of this structure.
The surgeon dissects the embolized right portal vein, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgical field reveals significant inflammation surrounding the portal vein, a result of previous portal vein embolization, with visible swollen and reddened tissue.
The surgeon encircles the right portal vein, applies clips, and transects it, completing the dissection of this vessel amidst the tense adhesions in the liver hilum.
The surgeon mobilizes the liver by transecting the falciform ligament in preparation for the right hemohepatectomy.
The surgeon mobilizes the liver from the inferior vena cava, clipping and transecting the short hepatic veins to free the liver from its attachments.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to verify the line of transection on the liver, ensuring accurate alignment for the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs the parenchymal transection of the liver, carefully separating the liver tissue along the predetermined line of transection.
Following the transection, the surgeon applies TachoSeal to the liver surface to achieve hemostasis, ensuring the area is free from bleeding.
The surgeon repairs the falciform ligament, which was previously transected during the liver mobilization process.
A silicone drain is placed at the end of the hepatectomy procedure.
Here, the perioperative data for stage I and II are shown.
Two-stage robotic liver surgery is a valid option for colorectal metastasis.
It offers a chance of cure and it can be performed safely in the hands of an experienced surgeon.
